The max pain price for Micron Technology (MU) options expiring tomorrow is $1,050.00. Some data sources estimate an alternative max pain of $1,012.50.Max pain is the strike price with the largest number of outstanding (open interest) call and put options. It is the price level where the maximum number of option contracts will expire worthless, resulting in the biggest financial loss for option buyers.Traders and market makers monitor this metric because option sellers tend to push the underlying stock price toward this level approaching expiration to maximize their own profits.

Hard buy? Hard pass? The market is deciding right now.    $MU    AI isn't just about GPUs. Every AI server needs massive amounts of memory, and that demand is becoming impossible to ignore.    Why I'm watching Micron:    • AI data centers require huge memory capacity per server, creating a real demand driver beyond the hype.  • Micron's business is diversified across AI, smartphones, autos, and everyday electronics.  • Memory is a cyclical industry. When demand outpaces supply, pricing power and margins can move quickly.    The biggest question isn't whether AI demand exists.    It's whether demand can keep growing faster than new supply comes online.    If the gap stays wide, the memory cycle could have more room to run.    But cycles work both ways, and supply expansion is always the risk.    I'm watching $MU closely. What's your take: hard buy or hard pass? $MU Memory Cycle Myth    Tech prices go down over time.  Production cost decline and demand and revenue increase.   The fact that memory prices don't decline in a straight line is the "Cycle" myth.      This is a logarithmic inflation adjusted (to 2024 USD) for a GB of memory.    The debate is if the uptrend will correct.  Historically yes, but it will be with increased volume, revenue and earnings.       BUT the trend appears to have broken dramatically.    No reasonable person can look at this and think that prices will get cheaper anytime soon in constant dollar terms.       10 years ago DDR4 was cheaper, we don't see this ever before in history where a memory type was more expensive after 10 years adjusted for inflation or dollar.
